title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: For questions 1–8, read the text below and choose which answer (A, B, C or D) best completes each gap.

content.text: Photography has (0) a long way since its invention in the early 19th century. The first photographs required extremely long exposure times, making it difficult to (1) moving subjects. Early photographers had to work with heavy equipment and dangerous chemicals. As technology advanced, cameras became smaller and more (2), allowing ordinary people to take their own pictures. The invention of color film in the 20th century was another major (3) that transformed the medium. However, nothing has changed photography more dramatically than the digital revolution. Digital cameras and smartphones have made it possible for anyone to take (4) photographs at virtually no cost. Social media platforms have also changed how we (5) and view images, with billions of photos shared online every day. Some argue that the ease of digital photography has (6) its value as an art form, while others believe it has democratized creative expression. Professional photographers have had to (7) their skills to remain competitive in this new landscape. Despite all these changes, the fundamental purpose of photography remains the same: to (8) moments and memories for future generations.

example.explanation: 'Come a long way' is a fixed expression that means having made a lot of progress.

questions.explanation: 'Capture' is the correct verb to describe the action of photographing subjects.
'Breakthrough' means significant breakthrough and is the most appropriate term for a major innovation.
'Breakthrough' means significant breakthrough and is the most appropriate term for a major innovation.
'Countless photographs' is the most natural collocation to express an innumerable quantity.
'Share images' is the standard placement in the social media context.
'Diminished' is the most appropriate verb to express a reduction in the perceived value of something.
'Adapt skills' is the correct collocation to describe the modification of skills in the face of new circumstances.
'Preserve moments' is the most appropriate collocation to describe the preservation of memories.
